comments.author: @JonfromsunnysarasotaFl
comments.text: I would love to visit Nauru but I heard it can be difficult because what you have to go through to visit. The biggest obstacle is a visa. I know me and my wife could get one it’s reaching out to the Nauru government in order for that to happen. Plus flights you have to fly into Australia or Fiji I believe is the only two countries that can take you the rest of the way. Correct if I’m wrong? Maybe one day as I’ve always wanted to see Nauru and also the Marshall Islands.

comments.author: @ForPropertyInvestors
comments.text: I went to high school with Jef Harris a native from Nauru. His family had sent him to a catholic boarding school in Hamilton, Victoria, Australia in the 80s. He was very shy and hide under the bed when he first arrived. Maybe he knew something about the catholic priests that we didn’t lol. I was a day student so wasn’t at boarding school. He wouldn’t go to the tuck shop, he used to give me a brand new 50 note EVERY day and ask me to buy things for him l’d buy lollies and things for him and bring the change back. $50 AUD in 1982 was a hell of a  lot for a kid to have. I used to get $2 from my own Australian mum and thought I was lucky most others didn’t. He said he was going to buy me a big rock box for doing him favours but he never did. You know the one they used carry on there shoulders during the wrap dancing yrs. Great big cast deck. Jef got the cuts a lot at school from getting in trouble. It used be called 6 of the best. Hand your hand out and get a Cain. He got in a fight and stomped on a guys balls one morning in the play hall i never heard a human being squeal so load. Monovue College Hamilton was the school. G’day Jef if you’re still alive. At school we used call the place Bird shit Island and knew of the wealth there because of stories from Jef. Though he wasn’t very fluent at talking very much at school, I don’t think he liked being around all these strange ppl being the only Naurian among all white Australians. We were nice and accommodating for him though. . 🎉

replies.author: @NovaTerrain-4K
replies.text: An incredibly deep and meaningful documentary! Nauru's story is not just a costly lesson about overexploiting natural resources, but also a testament to the incredible resilience of human nature. Despite all the ups and downs, the community bond and the love the locals have for their homeland truly touched my heart. Thank you for bringing us such an outstanding 4K video!
